stem: A 24-year-old man arrives in the trauma bay after a motorcycle collision with a suspected pelvic fracture and intra-abdominal hemorrhage. He is awake, diaphoretic, and tachycardic at 128 beats/min with a palpable radial pulse and a blood pressure of 88/56 mmHg. The trauma surgeon is mobilizing the team for emergent exploratory laparotomy and damage-control surgery, and the operating room is being readied. While the patient is being prepared for transport, which of the following resuscitation strategies is most appropriate before surgical hemorrhage control is achieved?

choices.text: Rapidly infuse 2 liters of warmed lactated Ringer's to normalize the blood pressure before transport to the operating room.
choices.rationale: Aggressive crystalloid bolus before bleeding is controlled dilutes clotting factors, displaces formed clot by raising pressure, worsens acidosis and hypothermia, and increases mortality, so it is the strategy to avoid.

choices.text: Titrate vasopressors to a mean arterial pressure above 90 mmHg while withholding blood products until the patient reaches the operating room.
choices.rationale: Vasopressors do not replace lost intravascular volume or oxygen-carrying capacity and driving pressure high before hemorrhage control promotes rebleeding, making this both ineffective and harmful.

choices.text: Delay all resuscitation and transfusion until a definitive CT angiogram localizes the bleeding source.
choices.rationale: A hemodynamically unstable hemorrhaging patient needs immediate blood product resuscitation and operative control, not a time-consuming imaging study that delays definitive treatment.

choices.text: Begin balanced transfusion of red cells, plasma, and platelets in an approximately 1:1:1 ratio while accepting permissive hypotension until bleeding is surgically controlled.
choices.rationale: Hemostatic resuscitation with a balanced 1:1:1 product ratio plus permissive hypotension restores oxygen-carrying capacity and coagulation factors while limiting clot disruption, and is the cornerstone of damage-control resuscitation.

choices.text: Transfuse packed red blood cells alone to a hemoglobin target of 10 g/dL without plasma or platelets.
choices.rationale: Giving red cells in isolation dilutes coagulation factors and platelets and precipitates the lethal triad of coagulopathy, acidosis, and hypothermia, so a red-cell-only strategy is inferior to balanced resuscitation.

explanation: Damage-control resuscitation rests on three pillars: hemorrhage control, permissive hypotension, and hemostatic (balanced) transfusion. Before the bleeding source is surgically controlled, the goal is to maintain enough perfusion to preserve vital organs (a palpable radial pulse or systolic pressure roughly 80-90 mmHg in a patient without traumatic brain injury) while avoiding the pressure-driven clot disruption and dilutional coagulopathy caused by large-volume crystalloid. Transfusing red cells, plasma, and platelets in an approximately 1:1:1 ratio mirrors whole blood and limits the coagulopathy-acidosis-hypothermia spiral that drives trauma mortality.
subtopic: Hemorrhagic shock - damage control resuscitation and permissive hypotension

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 3 - balanced 1:1:1 transfusion of red cells/plasma/platelets plus permissive hypotension until surgical hemorrhage control) is unambiguously the single best answer and is the textbook cornerstone of damage-control resuscitation for an unstable, actively hemorrhaging trauma patient with no TBI. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly explained: (A) 2L crystalloid bolus causes dilutional coagulopathy, clot disruption, and worsens the lethal triad; (B) vasopressors to MAP >90 do not restore volume/oxygen-carrying capacity and a high pressure promotes rebleeding; (C) delaying all resuscitation for CT angiogram is dangerous in a hemodynamically unstable patient who needs the OR; (E) PRBC-only to a Hgb of 10 causes dilutional coagulopathy and is inferior to balanced resuscitation (the Hgb 10 target is itself outdated vs the restrictive ~7 threshold, making it even more clearly wrong). The stem is internally consistent (SBP 88, palpable radial pulse, no TBI, heading to emergent laparotomy) and the permissive-hypotension explanation is appropriately caveated. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470382/ resolves to 'Hemorrhagic Shock - StatPearls,' which directly covers damage-control resuscitation, permissive hypotension (SBP ~90 mmHg target), hemostatic 1:1:1 transfusion, and avoidance of large-volume crystalloid - fully on-topic and supporting the keyed answer and explanation. Both clinical content and citation are sound.

stem: A 41-year-old construction worker is brought to the operating room for fasciotomy after a high-voltage electrical injury and prolonged extremity entrapment. He is hemodynamically stable, but the urine in his Foley catheter is dark tea-colored, the serum creatine kinase is 38,000 U/L, and the potassium is 5.6 mmol/L. Anesthesia is induced and the case proceeds. Which of the following is the most important intraoperative intervention to prevent acute kidney injury in this patient?

choices.text: Administer a loop diuretic to force diuresis and clear the pigment from the renal tubules.
choices.rationale: Loop diuretics are not first-line, may acidify the urine and promote myoglobin precipitation, and treating volume depletion with diuresis can worsen renal perfusion, so this is not the priority intervention.

choices.text: Begin aggressive intravenous crystalloid to establish and maintain a high urine output, targeting roughly 200-300 mL/hr.
choices.rationale: Early, aggressive volume expansion to drive a brisk urine output dilutes and flushes nephrotoxic myoglobin through the tubules, restores renal perfusion, and is the single most effective measure to prevent pigment-induced acute kidney injury.

choices.text: Restrict fluids to avoid pulmonary edema and rely on the kidneys to clear myoglobin once tissue perfusion improves.
choices.rationale: Fluid restriction in rhabdomyolysis allows concentrated myoglobin to precipitate in the tubules and leaves the patient volume depleted, directly increasing the risk of acute kidney injury.

choices.text: Transfuse packed red blood cells to dilute the circulating myoglobin and reduce its filtered load.
choices.rationale: Myoglobin is a freely filtered intracellular muscle protein unrelated to red cell mass, so transfusion does nothing to lower its concentration and is not indicated for renal protection.

choices.text: Give intravenous calcium to lower the serum potassium and thereby protect the kidneys.
choices.rationale: Calcium stabilizes the cardiac membrane against hyperkalemia but does not lower potassium or protect the kidney, and exogenous calcium should generally be avoided in early rhabdomyolysis because it can worsen later hypercalcemia.

explanation: High-voltage electrical and crush injuries cause extensive deep muscle necrosis and rhabdomyolysis; the released myoglobin precipitates in renal tubules, causes direct tubular toxicity and vasoconstriction, and produces pigment nephropathy. The dark urine, markedly elevated creatine kinase, and hyperkalemia are the classic picture. Early and aggressive volume resuscitation to maintain a high urine output (often 200-300 mL/hr) is the most important and best-supported intervention to dilute and clear myoglobin and preserve renal perfusion; bicarbonate-based urine alkalinization is a traditional adjunct, but volume expansion is the foundation.
subtopic: Crush/electrical injury - rhabdomyolysis and pigment nephropathy management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (aggressive IV crystalloid to maintain urine output ~200-300 mL/hr) is the single best, unambiguous answer for preventing pigment-induced AKI in crush/electrical rhabdomyolysis. Stem is a classic, internally consistent picture (myoglobinuria, CK 38,000, K+ 5.6). All four distractors are genuinely inferior: loop diuretics are not first-line and can acidify urine/promote myoglobin precipitation; fluid restriction is frankly harmful; PRBC transfusion is irrelevant since myoglobin is unrelated to RBC mass; IV calcium only stabilizes the cardiac membrane and exogenous calcium is appropriately discouraged early in rhabdomyolysis (risk of rebound hypercalcemia from sequestered calcium re-release). Rationales are accurate and current; volume expansion is correctly framed as the foundation with bicarbonate as an adjunct. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448168/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Rhabdomyolysis' article, directly on-topic, and explicitly supports the 200-300 mL/hr urine output target and aggressive early fluid resuscitation as the central AKI-prevention measure. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 33-year-old man with 45% total body surface area thermal burns is undergoing early tangential excision and split-thickness skin grafting on post-burn day 3. Two hours into the case, the esophageal temperature has drifted to 34.6 degrees C, the surgical field is oozing diffusely, and blood loss is brisk. Which of the following best explains why maintaining normothermia is a priority in this patient and identifies the most appropriate response?

choices.text: Hypothermia is protective in burn excision because it reduces metabolic demand, so the team should allow the temperature to remain low and continue operating.
choices.rationale: Permissive hypothermia is not appropriate here; in surgical bleeding it impairs the clotting cascade and platelet function and worsens hemorrhage rather than helping.

choices.text: The temperature drop is clinically unimportant because volatile anesthetics already abolish shivering, so no specific warming measures are needed.
choices.rationale: Abolishing shivering does not address the coagulopathy, increased transfusion need, and higher infection and mortality risk that accompany intraoperative hypothermia, so ignoring it is incorrect.

choices.text: Hypothermia should be corrected mainly to prevent postoperative myocardial ischemia, and the best step is to deepen the anesthetic to blunt the sympathetic response.
choices.rationale: While hypothermia can trigger adverse cardiac events on emergence, in this actively bleeding burn patient the dominant immediate concern is hypothermia-induced coagulopathy, and deepening anesthesia does not rewarm the patient.

choices.text: Hypothermia matters only for accurate temperature monitoring, so the correct action is to switch from an esophageal to a bladder temperature probe.
choices.rationale: Changing the monitoring site does not treat the hypothermia or its coagulopathic consequences and misidentifies the problem as a measurement artifact.

choices.text: Hypothermia worsens coagulopathy and increases blood loss, infection, and mortality, so the team should actively rewarm using forced-air warming, warmed intravenous fluids, and an operating room temperature of at least 25 degrees C.
choices.rationale: Burn patients lose thermoregulatory skin and have large exposed wounds, so hypothermia readily develops and impairs coagulation; aggressive active warming and a warm operating room are the correct, evidence-based response.

explanation: Patients undergoing burn excision lose their thermoregulatory skin barrier and expose large surface areas, making them highly prone to intraoperative hypothermia, which impairs the enzymatic clotting cascade and platelet function and increases blood loss, transfusion requirement, infection, and mortality. Early tangential excision is also associated with substantial blood loss (roughly 2.6% of blood volume per 1% TBSA excised in adults), so coagulopathy from hypothermia compounds an already bleeding field. The correct approach is aggressive normothermia maintenance: convective forced-air warming, warmed intravenous fluids, an ambient room temperature of 25 degrees C or higher, minimizing exposed surface, and accurate core temperature monitoring.
subtopic: Burn excision surgery - intraoperative thermoregulation, blood loss, and coagulopathy
reference_title: Anesthesia Considerations for Burn Surgery - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Keyed answer (index 4) is the single best choice — intraoperative hypothermia in burn excision impairs both the enzymatic coagulation cascade and platelet function, increases blood loss/transfusion need, and is associated with higher infection and mortality; the correct response bundle (forced-air convective warming, warmed IV fluids, ambient OR temp >=25C, minimizing exposed surface) is the evidence-based standard. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: (0) permissive hypothermia is false — it worsens surgical bleeding; (1) abolishing shivering does not address coagulopathy/transfusion/infection/mortality; (2) postop MI is a real but secondary concern and deepening anesthesia does not rewarm; (3) misframes the problem as a monitoring artifact — switching from esophageal to bladder probe would actually be a downgrade in core accuracy, especially with low urine output. The stem is realistic (day-3 early tangential excision is standard, 34.6C is clinically meaningful mild-moderate hypothermia). The 2.6%-of-blood-volume-per-1%-TBSA figure is accurate. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolved (valid page, last updated 12/21/2023) and is directly on-topic, corroborating the explanation almost verbatim — it states hypothermia increases coagulopathy/infection/mortality risk, gives the identical 2.6% adult (3.4% pediatric) blood-volume-per-1%-TBSA figure, and lists the same four warming strategies. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 71-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 38% predicted), prior radiation to the neck, and a documented Cormack-Lehane grade III view is already orotracheally intubated with an 8.0 single-lumen tube after an awake fiberoptic attempt. He now needs a right upper lobe wedge resection requiring left-lung ventilation and collapse of the right lung. Switching to a double-lumen tube is judged unsafe given his difficult airway. Which lung-isolation strategy best fits this situation?

choices.text: Extubate and re-attempt placement of a left-sided double-lumen tube using direct laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Re-instrumenting a known difficult grade III airway to exchange for a bulkier double-lumen tube needlessly risks losing the airway and is not the safest option here.

choices.text: Advance a left-sided double-lumen tube over an airway exchange catheter and remove the single-lumen tube
choices.rationale: Tube exchange over a catheter in a difficult airway carries real risk of catheter dislodgement and loss of the airway, and the larger double-lumen tube remains the more traumatic choice when a blocker is available.

choices.text: Pass a bronchial blocker through the existing single-lumen tube and position it in the right mainstem bronchus under fiberoptic guidance
choices.rationale: A bronchial blocker deploys through the in-situ single-lumen tube without re-instrumenting the difficult airway and isolates the right lung, making it the safest effective choice.

choices.text: Use deliberate endobronchial intubation by advancing the single-lumen tube into the left mainstem bronchus
choices.rationale: Blind left-mainstem advancement of a single-lumen tube gives an unreliable seal, risks occluding the left upper lobe, and does not reliably collapse the operative lung.

choices.text: Proceed with two-lung ventilation and ask the surgeon to retract the inflated lung
choices.rationale: Two-lung ventilation does not provide the lung collapse needed for the resection and is poorly tolerated surgically, so it does not solve the isolation problem.

explanation: In a patient who is already intubated through a difficult airway, a bronchial blocker is the lung-isolation device of choice because it can be advanced through the existing single-lumen endotracheal tube and positioned under fiberoptic guidance without re-instrumenting the airway. Blockers also permit selective lobar isolation. Their trade-offs are slower lung collapse, greater dislodgement risk, and difficulty sealing the right upper lobe, but those are acceptable when exchanging for a double-lumen tube would jeopardize a known difficult airway.
subtopic: Lung isolation device selection — bronchial blocker in the difficult airway
reference_title: Lung Isolation Anesthesia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 2 - bronchial blocker through the existing single-lumen tube, positioned under fiberoptic guidance) is unambiguously the single best answer. In a patient already intubated through a known difficult airway (CL grade III, prior neck radiation, awake fiberoptic), avoiding re-instrumentation is the safest path, and a blocker deploys through the in-situ 8.0 SLT (which easily accommodates blocker + bronchoscope). All distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly reasoned: (A) re-DLT via DL risks losing the airway; (B) DLT exchange over a catheter is hazardous in a difficult airway; (D) blind left endobronchial SLT advancement gives an unreliable seal and risks LUL occlusion; (E) two-lung ventilation provides no collapse. The explanation is accurate and notably honest about the blocker's real trade-offs (slower collapse, higher dislodgement risk, and the well-known difficulty sealing the RUL when the blocker sits in the right mainstem) without those caveats undermining the keyed choice. Current and correct. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK555933/ resolves to StatPearls 'Lung Isolation Anesthesia,' which directly covers bronchial blockers advanced through a standard ETT, blocker-vs-DLT trade-offs (slower collapse, better DLT deflation/less repositioning), blocker placement under fiberoptic guidance, and isolation in difficult-airway/already-intubated scenarios. Citation is real, resolves, and is on-topic.

stem: A 68-year-old man undergoes open repair of a Crawford extent II thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm with a prolonged supraceliac cross-clamp. A lumbar cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) drain was placed preoperatively. On emergence he has bilateral lower-extremity flaccid paralysis and loss of pain and temperature sensation below the level of injury, but vibration and proprioception are preserved. Mean arterial pressure is 62 mm Hg and CSF pressure reads 16 mm Hg. Which intervention most directly improves spinal cord perfusion pressure in this anterior spinal artery syndrome?

choices.text: Begin therapeutic hypothermia to 32°C to reduce cerebral metabolic rate
choices.rationale: Systemic deep hypothermia is not the targeted bedside maneuver for an established deficit and does not directly change the spinal cord perfusion pressure equation.

choices.text: Administer high-dose corticosteroids to reduce cord edema
choices.rationale: Steroids have no proven benefit for ischemic spinal cord injury after aortic repair and do not address the perfusion-pressure deficit.

choices.text: Transfuse to a hemoglobin of 12 g/dL to maximize oxygen-carrying capacity
choices.rationale: Raising hemoglobin improves oxygen content but does not raise the mean arterial pressure minus CSF pressure gradient that defines cord perfusion.

choices.text: Drain CSF to lower CSF pressure and raise the mean arterial pressure to augment spinal cord perfusion pressure
choices.rationale: Because spinal cord perfusion pressure equals mean distal arterial pressure minus CSF pressure, draining CSF and raising MAP directly widens that gradient and is the standard rescue for this deficit.

choices.text: Give nitroprusside to lower afterload and reduce aortic graft wall tension
choices.rationale: Lowering MAP would worsen the perfusion gradient and deepen the ischemic injury, which is the opposite of what is needed.

explanation: Anterior spinal artery (ASA) syndrome after thoracoabdominal aortic repair reflects ischemia of the anterior two-thirds of the cord, typically from compromise of the artery of Adamkiewicz, producing motor loss and loss of pain/temperature with preserved dorsal-column function (proprioception and vibration). Spinal cord perfusion pressure equals mean distal arterial pressure minus CSF pressure, so the immediate management of a new deficit is to drain CSF (lowering CSF pressure) and raise MAP to widen that gradient. Maintaining MAP above roughly 80-90 mm Hg with concurrent CSF drainage is the accepted rescue strategy.
subtopic: Thoracoabdominal aortic repair — spinal cord protection and anterior cord syndrome
reference_title: Spinal Cord Ischemia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 3) is the single best and unambiguous answer. The stem describes classic anterior spinal artery syndrome after Crawford extent II TAAA repair (motor loss + loss of pain/temperature with preserved dorsal-column proprioception/vibration). Spinal cord perfusion pressure = mean distal arterial pressure − CSF pressure, so draining CSF (CSF pressure is elevated at 16 mmHg, above the usual <10-12 target) and raising the low MAP (62) directly widens the gradient — the accepted rescue strategy. All distractors are genuinely inferior: therapeutic hypothermia is not the targeted bedside maneuver and does not change the SCPP equation; corticosteroids have no proven benefit for ischemic spinal cord injury after aortic repair; transfusing to Hgb 12 raises oxygen content but not the MAP−CSF gradient (and a liberal target of 12 is not standard); nitroprusside lowers MAP and would worsen/deepen the ischemia. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current; the MAP >80-90 mmHg rescue target is consistent with guideline practice.

REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539870/ resolves successfully to the StatPearls "Spinal Cord Ischemia" article, which explicitly covers anterior spinal artery syndrome, the CSF-pressure/spinal cord perfusion relationship, lumbar CSF drainage as augmentation of spinal cord perfusion pressure, and MAP maintenance after thoracoabdominal aortic repair. Citation is on-topic and supports the keyed answer. Both clinical and reference are sound -> keep.

stem: A 76-year-old woman with a 5.7 cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm and significant coronary disease undergoes endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) under monitored anesthesia care with a femoral approach. Completion angiography after stent-graft deployment shows contrast filling the residual aneurysm sac with retrograde flow originating from a patent lumbar artery; the proximal and distal seal zones and graft components appear intact, and the sac is not enlarging. Which type of endoleak is this?

choices.text: Type I endoleak from an inadequate proximal or distal seal
choices.rationale: A type I leak arises at the attachment (seal) zones, but here the seal zones are intact and flow comes from a side branch, so this is not type I.

choices.text: Type II endoleak from retrograde flow through a patent lumbar artery
choices.rationale: Retrograde sac filling from a patent lumbar or inferior mesenteric artery with intact seal zones and components is the definition of a type II endoleak.

choices.text: Type III endoleak from separation or fracture of graft components
choices.rationale: A type III leak results from component separation or fabric defect, which is explicitly excluded because the graft components are intact.

choices.text: Type IV endoleak from graft fabric porosity
choices.rationale: A type IV leak is diffuse transudation through porous graft fabric, not focal retrograde flow from a named branch vessel.

choices.text: Type V endoleak (endotension) with sac expansion and no identifiable leak source
choices.rationale: Type V/endotension is continued sac pressurization without a demonstrable leak source, whereas here an identifiable lumbar-artery source is seen and the sac is not enlarging.

explanation: Endoleaks after EVAR are classified by their source: type I at the proximal/distal seal (attachment) zones, type II from retrograde collateral flow via patent lumbar or inferior mesenteric arteries, type III from component separation or graft defect, type IV from fabric porosity, and type V (endotension) as sac pressurization without a visible leak. The scenario describes retrograde filling from a patent lumbar artery with intact seal zones and components, which is a type II endoleak. Type II leaks with a stable, non-enlarging sac are commonly observed with surveillance imaging, in contrast to type I and III leaks, which are high-pressure and generally require prompt intervention.
subtopic: Endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) — endoleak classification
reference_title: Abdominal Aortic Repair - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (Type II endoleak) is the single best and unambiguous answer. The stem describes retrograde sac filling from a patent lumbar artery with intact seal zones and intact graft components and a non-enlarging sac — this is the textbook definition of a Type II endoleak (retrograde collateral flow via lumbar or IMA). All distractors are genuinely inferior and are explicitly excluded by stem details: Type I requires a seal-zone defect (stem says seals intact), Type III requires component separation/fabric defect (stem says components intact), Type IV is diffuse fabric porosity not focal named-branch flow, and Type V (endotension) requires no identifiable source plus pressurization (here a source is identified and the sac is stable). All rationales are accurate. The explanation's classification (I-V) and the management pearl (Type II with stable sac is surveilled; Type I/III are high-pressure and need prompt intervention) are correct and current per SVS guidance. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554573/ resolves to 'Abdominal Aortic Repair - StatPearls,' which is exactly on-topic and explicitly states 'Type II endoleak - blood flow from patent collateral arteries (lumbar or inferior mesenteric arteries) to the aneurysmal sac,' directly supporting the keyed answer. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 78-year-old, 60-kg woman undergoes an ultrasound-guided interscalene brachial plexus block with 30 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine for shoulder surgery. About 5 minutes after injection she becomes agitated, complains of perioral numbness and tinnitus, then has a generalized seizure followed by a wide-complex bradycardic rhythm with a blood pressure of 62/30 mm Hg. Seizure activity is controlled and the airway is secured with 100% oxygen. In addition to standard resuscitation, what is the most important next pharmacologic intervention?

choices.text: Administer a 20% lipid emulsion bolus of 1.5 mL/kg over 1 minute, followed by an infusion of 0.25 mL/kg/min
choices.rationale: Intravenous lipid emulsion is the definitive antidote for local anesthetic systemic toxicity and should be started promptly once toxicity with cardiovascular instability is recognized.

choices.text: Give a high-dose epinephrine bolus of 1 mg IV per standard adult ACLS dosing
choices.rationale: In LAST, large epinephrine boluses (>1 mcg/kg) can impair resuscitation and worsen outcomes, so reduced doses are recommended rather than the standard 1 mg bolus.

choices.text: Administer a calcium channel blocker such as verapamil to control the rhythm
choices.rationale: Calcium channel blockers and beta-blockers are specifically avoided in LAST because they further depress already-impaired myocardial conduction and contractility.

choices.text: Give intravenous flumazenil to reverse the central nervous system effects
choices.rationale: Flumazenil is a benzodiazepine antagonist and has no role against local anesthetic toxicity, which is not mediated by GABA-receptor agonism.

choices.text: Administer procainamide to treat the wide-complex rhythm
choices.rationale: Procainamide is a sodium-channel-blocking (Class Ia) antiarrhythmic and is contraindicated in LAST because it compounds the sodium-channel blockade already causing the toxicity.

explanation: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) classically progresses from CNS excitation (perioral numbness, tinnitus, agitation, seizures) to cardiovascular collapse, and bupivacaine is the most cardiotoxic agent because of its avid, slowly-dissociating sodium-channel binding. The ASRA-endorsed antidote is 20% lipid emulsion, given as a 1.5 mL/kg bolus over 1 minute followed by a 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion, which creates a 'lipid sink' and supports myocardial energetics. Standard ACLS is modified in LAST: epinephrine doses are reduced (<1 mcg/kg), and vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmics are avoided.
subtopic: Local anesthetics — lipid emulsion therapy for LAST

verdict.reason: Clinical content is sound and the key is unambiguous. The stem is a textbook LAST presentation (interscalene block, 30 mL 0.5% bupivacaine = 150 mg / 2.5 mg/kg in a 60-kg patient, likely intravascular; CNS prodrome of perioral numbness/tinnitus/agitation -> seizure -> wide-complex bradycardia + hypotension; bupivacaine is the most cardiotoxic agent). The keyed answer A (20% lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg bolus + 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion) is the single best answer per ASRA guidance for LAST with cardiovascular instability. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly explained: standard 1 mg epinephrine (ASRA recommends reduced dosing <1 mcg/kg), verapamil/CCB (avoided), flumazenil (no role; LAST is not GABA-mediated), and procainamide (Class Ia Na-channel blocker, contraindicated). The explanation accurately captures pathophysiology, the lipid-sink mechanism, and modified ACLS principles. Minor, non-disqualifying nuance: answer A states the bolus is given 'over 1 minute,' whereas current ASRA/StatPearls phrasing is 'over 2-3 minutes' (the bolus volume 1.5 mL/kg and infusion 0.25 mL/kg/min are correct, and 'over 1 minute' is a long-published historical value). This does not alter which option is best. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499964/ resolves to 'Local Anesthetic Toxicity (StatPearls)' and directly covers LAST, lipid emulsion therapy, bupivacaine cardiotoxicity, and reduced epinephrine dosing — fully on-topic and title-matched.

stem: A healthy 24-year-old woman undergoes laparoscopic appendectomy under general anesthesia. Deep neuromuscular blockade with rocuronium is reversed at the end of the case with sugammadex 4 mg/kg, with a return of the train-of-four ratio to greater than 0.9. During emergence planning, the anesthesia care team reviews her medications and notes that her only regular medication is a combined oral contraceptive pill. What counseling regarding her contraception is most appropriate?

choices.text: Sugammadex may bind progestogen, so she should use an additional non-hormonal contraceptive method for 7 days as if she had missed a dose
choices.rationale: Sugammadex encapsulates progestogen and is considered equivalent to one missed daily contraceptive dose, requiring backup contraception for 7 days.

choices.text: No additional precaution is needed because sugammadex does not interact with any hormonal contraceptives
choices.rationale: This is incorrect because sugammadex's cyclodextrin structure can bind progestogen and reduce contraceptive efficacy.

choices.text: She should stop her oral contraceptive permanently because sugammadex causes irreversible binding of the hormone
choices.rationale: The interaction is a single transient capture event, not a permanent effect, so discontinuing the contraceptive is unwarranted.

choices.text: She needs backup contraception only if she also received neostigmine for reversal
choices.rationale: Neostigmine has no relevant interaction with hormonal contraception; the concern is specific to sugammadex's encapsulation of progestogen.

choices.text: She should take a double dose of her oral contraceptive the next day to compensate
choices.rationale: Doubling the dose is not the recommended management; the guidance is to use backup non-hormonal contraception for 7 days.

explanation: Sugammadex is a modified gamma-cyclodextrin that reverses aminosteroid neuromuscular blockers (rocuronium, vecuronium) by encapsulating them in a 1:1 complex. The same hydrophobic cavity can also capture progestogen, and a single dose of sugammadex is considered equivalent to missing one daily dose of an oral contraceptive. Patients on hormonal contraception (oral, or non-oral methods that rely on progestogen) should therefore be advised to use an additional non-hormonal contraceptive method for the following 7 days.
subtopic: Sugammadex — interaction with hormonal contraception

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Sugammadex is a modified gamma-cyclodextrin that encapsulates aminosteroid NMBAs in a 1:1 complex; its cavity can also capture the progestogen component of hormonal contraceptives. Per the manufacturer's SmPC/label, a single bolus dose equals one missed daily contraceptive dose, requiring an additional non-hormonal method for 7 days. Keyed answer (index 0) states exactly this. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: no interaction (false), irreversible/permanent binding requiring permanent discontinuation (false — transient single capture), backup only if neostigmine given (false — neostigmine has no such interaction), and double-dosing the OCP (not the recommended management). The 4 mg/kg dose for deep-block reversal and TOF >0.9 endpoint are clinically appropriate. REFERENCE: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470263/ resolves to the StatPearls "Sugammadex" chapter and explicitly addresses the contraceptive interaction, stating sugammadex binds progesterone and reduces contraceptive efficacy, with a recommendation for 7 days of nonhormonal backup equivalent to one missed dose — directly on-topic and corroborating the key.

stem: A 68-year-old man with Parkinson disease, who has been taking reserpine-like dopamine-depleting therapy and has long-standing depleted catecholamine stores, undergoes spinal anesthesia for a transurethral resection of the prostate. Shortly after the block sets up he becomes hypotensive (BP 78/44 mm Hg, HR 58/min). The provider gives a 10-mg IV bolus of ephedrine, but the blood pressure response is minimal; a repeated bolus produces even less effect. Which mechanism best explains the blunted and progressively diminishing response to ephedrine in this patient?

choices.text: Ephedrine acts largely indirectly by releasing stored norepinephrine, and his depleted presynaptic catecholamine stores leave little to release
choices.rationale: Ephedrine is predominantly an indirect-acting sympathomimetic that displaces norepinephrine from presynaptic vesicles, so depleted stores blunt its effect and repeated dosing causes tachyphylaxis.

choices.text: Ephedrine selectively blocks alpha-1 receptors, so vasodilation predominates in this patient
choices.rationale: Ephedrine is a sympathomimetic agonist, not an alpha-1 antagonist, so this mischaracterizes its pharmacology.

choices.text: Spinal anesthesia accelerates hepatic metabolism of ephedrine, lowering its plasma concentration
choices.rationale: Spinal anesthesia does not meaningfully increase ephedrine metabolism, and pharmacokinetics do not explain the depleted-store phenomenon.

choices.text: Ephedrine works only on cardiac beta-1 receptors and cannot raise blood pressure
choices.rationale: Ephedrine has both alpha and beta activity and can raise blood pressure, so this is not the explanation for its failure here.

choices.text: Dopamine depletion upregulates postsynaptic adrenergic receptors, causing a paradoxical loss of response to all vasopressors
choices.rationale: Chronic depletion tends to upregulate (sensitize) postsynaptic receptors, which would favor direct agonists rather than abolish all vasopressor response, so this does not explain the ephedrine failure.

explanation: Ephedrine raises blood pressure mainly through an indirect mechanism: it enters the presynaptic neuron and displaces stored norepinephrine into the synapse (with a smaller direct receptor effect). In patients with depleted catecholamine stores—from reserpine-type drugs, chronic dopamine depletion, or repeated dosing—there is little norepinephrine left to release, so the response is blunted and rapidly diminishes (tachyphylaxis). The rational alternative is a direct-acting agent such as phenylephrine, whose effect does not depend on endogenous norepinephrine stores and may even be exaggerated because of postsynaptic receptor upregulation.
subtopic: Sympathomimetics — ephedrine indirect mechanism and tachyphylaxis

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 0) is the single best answer and is correct — ephedrine is predominantly an indirect-acting sympathomimetic (displaces/releases stored NE, inhibits reuptake) with a smaller direct effect; reserpine-type depletion of presynaptic catecholamine stores plus repeated dosing produces blunted response and tachyphylaxis. Standard board teaching (Stoelting, Miller) confirms phenylephrine, a direct agonist, is the appropriate rescue. All four distractors are genuinely and clearly inferior: B falsely calls ephedrine an alpha-1 antagonist; C invokes implausible PK (ephedrine is largely renally excreted unchanged, not accelerated hepatic metabolism); D wrongly limits ephedrine to beta-1; E is internally contradictory (upregulation would enhance, not abolish, vasopressor response). Rationales are accurate. Only a minor non-disqualifying nitpick: reserpine is not a real Parkinson therapy and reserpine's classic teaching concerns peripheral NE rather than dopamine depletion, but the stem hedges ('reserpine-like... depleted catecholamine stores') and this does not create answer ambiguity. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK547661/ resolves to 'Ephedrine - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf,' which explicitly states ephedrine acts as both a direct and indirect sympathomimetic, displaces NE from storage vesicles, and that 'tachyphylaxis will develop with prolonged and repeated use as this depletes endogenous norepinephrine stores' — directly on-topic and supportive of the keyed mechanism.

stem: A previously healthy 18-month-old is brought to the OR for rigid bronchoscopy after a witnessed choking episode while eating peanuts, followed by coughing and intermittent wheeze. He is now stable on room air with mild expiratory wheeze over the right lung field and a chest radiograph showing right-sided hyperinflation. The otolaryngologist will share the airway via the rigid bronchoscope. Which anesthetic approach best matches the goals of this shared-airway, partially obstructing foreign-body case?

choices.text: Rapid sequence induction with succinylcholine and immediate positive-pressure mask ventilation before scope insertion
choices.rationale: Aggressive positive-pressure ventilation before the object is controlled can drive a mobile foreign body more distally or convert a partial to a complete obstruction, and paralysis-dependent apnea is risky in a shared airway.

choices.text: Inhalational induction maintaining spontaneous ventilation, with the surgeon ready to instrument the airway and deepen anesthesia via the bronchoscope side-port
choices.rationale: Correct: preserving spontaneous ventilation avoids forcing the object distally and maintains gas exchange while the rigid scope is introduced, which is the classic approach for an obstructing airway foreign body.

choices.text: Awake placement of a supraglottic airway followed by foreign-body retrieval through the device
choices.rationale: A supraglottic airway sits above the glottis and cannot provide the rigid conduit, ventilation, or distal instrumentation needed to retrieve a bronchial foreign body.

choices.text: Defer the procedure for 24 hours of nebulized bronchodilators and steroids to shrink airway edema first
choices.rationale: An aspirated organic foreign body warrants timely removal; delay risks migration, mucosal inflammation, and post-obstructive complications rather than improving conditions.

choices.text: Total intravenous anesthesia with full neuromuscular blockade and apneic oxygenation throughout the case
choices.rationale: Mandatory apnea with full paralysis removes the safety margin of spontaneous breathing in a shared airway where the surgeon intermittently occludes the lumen, increasing desaturation risk.

explanation: Rigid bronchoscopy under general anesthesia is the definitive treatment for a suspected airway foreign body, and the rigid scope itself allows ventilation, superior visualization, and instrumentation. The traditional anesthetic priority in an obstructing or mobile foreign body is to maintain spontaneous ventilation during induction so that positive pressure does not advance the object or worsen obstruction, while anesthesia is deepened (often with volatile agent plus topical airway local anesthetic) through the bronchoscope. Close, continuous communication with the surgeon over the shared airway is essential.
subtopic: Pediatric airway foreign body — rigid bronchoscopy anesthetic management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound. The stem (18-mo, witnessed peanut aspiration, stable on RA, right-sided wheeze + right-sided hyperinflation = ball-valve partial obstruction, shared airway via rigid scope) is internally consistent and points cleanly to the board-classic answer B: inhalational induction maintaining spontaneous ventilation with the surgeon ready and anesthesia deepened via the scope. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: A (PPV before control) risks advancing/completing the obstruction; C (awake SGA in a toddler, retrieval through SGA) is nonsensical and cannot provide a rigid conduit; D (24h delay for an organic/peanut FB) is contraindicated. The strongest adversarial challenge is E vs B, since spontaneous vs controlled ventilation for airway FB is genuinely debated. However, E is deliberately written as an inferior variant — full neuromuscular blockade with mandatory apneic oxygenation throughout the entire case — which is not the balanced controlled-ventilation technique that competes with SV, and removes the safety margin in an intermittently occluded shared airway. So B remains the single best/board-standard answer; the case is not truly two-defensible-answer ambiguous. Keyed and distractor rationales are accurate and current; the explanation's mention of volatile + topical airway local anesthetic to preserve SV is correct. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK531480/ resolves to StatPearls 'Foreign Body Aspiration,' genuinely on-topic, and explicitly supports the core claims (bronchoscopy as definitive management; rigid bronchoscopy's ventilation, visualization, and instrumentation advantages). It is marked 'Archived' and does not itself detail anesthetic management/spontaneous ventilation, but it is not fabricated, 404, or mismatched, so this is not a reference problem. Both prongs pass.

stem: A 44-year-old woman is recovering on the surgical ward roughly 36 hours after an uncomplicated total thyroidectomy. She develops perioral tingling and cramping in her hands, then progresses to inspiratory stridor and increased work of breathing. Her neck dressing is flat and non-expanding with no swelling or ecchymosis, and her voice is normal. On exam she has a positive Chvostek sign. Which intervention most directly addresses the underlying cause of her airway compromise?

choices.text: Immediate bedside opening of the surgical incision to evacuate a neck hematoma
choices.rationale: Wound evacuation treats an expanding hematoma, but her flat, non-expanding dressing and the timing/tetany features point away from hematoma as the cause.

choices.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate to correct acute hypocalcemia
choices.rationale: Correct: perioral paresthesia, carpal cramping, a positive Chvostek sign, and delayed-onset stridor indicate hypocalcemic laryngospasm from post-thyroidectomy hypoparathyroidism, which IV calcium directly reverses.

choices.text: Urgent re-exploration for presumed bilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury
choices.rationale: Bilateral RLN injury typically causes stridor with a hoarse or absent voice immediately after extubation, not a normal voice with tetanic signs 36 hours later.

choices.text: Nebulized racemic epinephrine and dexamethasone for post-extubation subglottic edema
choices.rationale: Airway edema would present much earlier, near the time of extubation, and would not explain the systemic neuromuscular irritability seen here.

choices.text: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for a deep neck space infection
choices.rationale: There are no signs of infection (fever, erythema, fluctuance) and the tetany plus normal voice make hypocalcemia, not abscess, the explanation.

explanation: Stridor after thyroidectomy has a broad differential—neck hematoma, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, tracheomalacia, and hypocalcemia. Hypocalcemia from inadvertent parathyroid injury characteristically appears 24–72 hours postoperatively with perioral paresthesia, carpopedal spasm, and positive Chvostek/Trousseau signs, and can cause laryngospasm and stridor; the clue here is tetany with a flat dressing and a normal voice. IV calcium is the targeted treatment, with airway support as needed while it takes effect.
subtopic: Post-thyroidectomy airway compromise — hypocalcemic laryngospasm vs. hematoma

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (B, IV calcium gluconate) is the single best answer. The vignette gives a textbook post-thyroidectomy hypocalcemia picture: onset at 36h (classic 24-72h window for parathyroid injury/devascularization), perioral paresthesia, carpal cramping, and a positive Chvostek sign indicating neuromuscular irritability, with hypocalcemic laryngospasm producing stridor. The stem asks for the intervention that "most directly addresses the underlying cause," and IV calcium corrects the root hypocalcemia; the explanation appropriately notes airway support as a temporizing adjunct without overstating. All distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly excluded by discriminating findings: (A) hematoma excluded by flat/non-expanding dressing, no ecchymosis, and earlier typical timing (75% within first 6h); (C) bilateral RLN injury excluded by normal voice and onset 36h later rather than immediately post-extubation; (D) subglottic edema excluded by late timing and unexplained systemic tetany; (E) deep neck infection excluded by absence of fever/erythema/fluctuance. No ambiguity—"underlying cause" wording cleanly favors calcium over mere airway management. REFERENCE: The reference_url resolves (HTTP 200) to the OpenAnesthesia "Anesthesia for Thyroidectomy" page, which is directly on-topic and explicitly covers all four differential items used in the stem/explanation—hypocalcemia (cited as 24-72h post-op, matching the vignette), recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, neck hematoma (75% in first 6h), and tracheomalacia. Citation is valid and well-matched.

stem: A 44-year-old woman with episodic headaches, palpitations, and diaphoresis is scheduled for laparoscopic resection of a 5-cm right adrenal mass after plasma free metanephrines confirmed a pheochromocytoma. Her cardiologist started her on metoprolol 50 mg twice daily three days ago for a heart rate in the 110s, and she now presents to your preoperative clinic. Her blood pressure today is 188/112 mmHg, heart rate 78/min, and she reports a new severe pounding headache that began the day after the metoprolol was started. What is the most appropriate next step in optimizing this patient before surgery?

choices.text: Continue the metoprolol and add intravenous labetalol to achieve tighter heart-rate control
choices.rationale: Adding more beta-blockade compounds the underlying problem of unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction and does not address the worsening hypertension.

choices.text: Proceed to surgery now, since heart-rate control has been achieved and tumor removal will definitively treat the hypertension
choices.rationale: Operating on an inadequately alpha-blocked, volume-contracted patient invites a life-threatening intraoperative hypertensive crisis and is unacceptable.

choices.text: Stop the metoprolol, begin an alpha-antagonist such as phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin, and add a beta-blocker back only after alpha-blockade is established
choices.rationale: Alpha-blockade is the mainstay of preparation and must precede beta-blockade; her hypertension worsened precisely because beta-blockade was started first, producing unopposed alpha stimulation.

choices.text: Add oral clonidine to suppress central catecholamine output and reassess in one week
choices.rationale: Clonidine does not reliably control catecholamine-driven crises from an autonomous tumor and abrupt withdrawal can cause rebound hypertension, so it is not the appropriate primary preparation.

choices.text: Restrict dietary sodium and fluids to reduce blood pressure before surgery
choices.rationale: These patients are typically volume-contracted and actually require a high-sodium diet and liberal fluids during alpha-blockade to expand intravascular volume and prevent post-resection hypotension.

explanation: In pheochromocytoma, beta-blockade must never precede alpha-blockade: blocking beta-2-mediated vasodilation while alpha receptors remain unopposed leaves catecholamine-driven vasoconstriction unchecked and can precipitate severe hypertension, as occurred when this patient's metoprolol was started first. Correct preparation is to establish alpha-blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) over roughly 10-14 days with concurrent sodium and volume loading, adding a beta-blocker only afterward for persistent tachycardia. Adequacy is judged by criteria such as blood pressure below 160/90 for 24 hours and absence of orthostatic instability before proceeding.
subtopic: Pheochromocytoma - preoperative alpha- and beta-blockade sequencing

verdict.reason: Clinical content is sound and the keyed answer (index 2: stop metoprolol, establish alpha-blockade with phenoxybenzamine/doxazosin, add beta-blocker only afterward) is the single best, unambiguous answer. The stem is a clean presentation of unopposed alpha stimulation from premature beta-blockade (worsening HTN 188/112 and new headache the day after metoprolol). All distractors are genuinely inferior: (0) labetalol is predominantly a beta-antagonist (~7:1 beta:alpha oral) and worsens unopposed alpha; (1) proceeding on an inadequately alpha-blocked, volume-contracted patient invites intraoperative crisis; (3) clonidine is not reliable primary prep for an autonomous tumor; (4) sodium/fluid restriction is the opposite of the required high-sodium volume loading. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current (Roizen-type BP <160/90 for 24h, 10-14 day prep, volume expansion). Minor pharmacologic nuance: metoprolol is beta-1 selective, so the explanation's "blocking beta-2 vasodilation" mechanism is most precise for non-selective agents, but the core board principle (never beta before alpha) is unambiguously correct and not answer-altering. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/pheochromocytoma/ resolves and is directly on-topic, explicitly stating alpha-blockers are the mainstay, that beta-blockade should never precede alpha-blockade due to unopposed alpha stimulation, high-sodium/volume loading, 10-14 day timeline, and Roizen criteria.

stem: A 29-year-old woman with poorly controlled Graves disease undergoes emergent open appendectomy for perforated appendicitis; there was no time for preoperative antithyroid optimization. About 45 minutes into the case, her temperature rises to 39.8 C, heart rate climbs to 165/min in atrial fibrillation, and her blood pressure falls to 84/46 mmHg. The surgical field shows no bleeding to explain the instability, end-tidal CO2 and airway pressures are normal, and there is no rash or wheezing. Which intervention most directly targets the underlying pathophysiology of this crisis?

choices.text: Administer dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg and discontinue volatile anesthetic for presumed malignant hyperthermia
choices.rationale: The absence of a rising end-tidal CO2, muscle rigidity, and metabolic/respiratory acidosis argues against malignant hyperthermia, and dantrolene does not treat thyrotoxicosis.

choices.text: Give an intravenous beta-blocker such as esmolol or propranolol, then add a thionamide (propylthiouracil or methimazole), hydrocortisone, and active cooling
choices.rationale: This is the directed treatment of thyroid storm: beta-blockade controls the adrenergic surge and tachyarrhythmia while thionamides block hormone synthesis, corticosteroids reduce peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion, and cooling addresses hyperthermia.

choices.text: Administer intravenous levothyroxine to correct a presumed acute thyroid hormone deficiency
choices.rationale: Giving exogenous thyroid hormone would worsen an already hyperthyroid, thyrotoxic state and is exactly the wrong direction of therapy.

choices.text: Treat as anaphylaxis with intramuscular epinephrine, antihistamines, and corticosteroids
choices.rationale: There is no urticaria, bronchospasm, or temporal antigen exposure, and epinephrine would intensify the catecholamine-driven tachyarrhythmia and hyperthermia.

choices.text: Give amiodarone as the first-line agent to chemically cardiovert the atrial fibrillation
choices.rationale: Amiodarone is iodine-rich and can aggravate thyrotoxicosis, and rate control with a beta-blocker plus treating the underlying storm is preferred over making it the primary intervention.

explanation: Thyroid storm classically presents intraoperatively as the triad of hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia (often atrial fibrillation), and hemodynamic instability in a patient with untreated or undertreated hyperthyroidism subjected to surgical stress; it is a key differential for intraoperative hyperthermia alongside malignant hyperthermia, which it can mimic. Treatment is multimodal and targets each step of the pathophysiology: beta-blockade for the adrenergic surge, a thionamide to halt new hormone synthesis, corticosteroids to blunt peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion and treat possible relative adrenal insufficiency, and aggressive cooling. Distinguishing it from malignant hyperthermia hinges on the absence of a rising end-tidal CO2 and acidosis.
subtopic: Thyroid storm - intraoperative recognition and management

verdict.reason: Clinical: Stem is a classic intraoperative thyroid storm (poorly controlled Graves, emergent unoptimized surgery, hyperthermia 39.8 C, AF at 165, hypotension) and deliberately excludes the key mimics — MH (normal EtCO2/airway pressures, no rigidity/acidosis), surgical bleeding, and anaphylaxis (no rash/wheeze). Keyed answer B (IV beta-blocker [esmolol/propranolol] + thionamide [PTU/methimazole] + hydrocortisone + active cooling) is the textbook directed therapy and is unambiguously the single best answer; the rationale chain (beta-blockade for adrenergic surge/AF, thionamide to halt synthesis, steroids to blunt peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion and cover relative adrenal insufficiency, cooling) is accurate and current. Distractors are genuinely inferior: A dantrolene/MH (excluded by normal EtCO2 and no acidosis; dantrolene doesn't treat thyrotoxicosis), C levothyroxine (wrong direction, worsens thyrotoxicosis), D anaphylaxis/epinephrine (no allergic features; epinephrine worsens the catecholamine crisis), E amiodarone (iodine-rich, can aggravate storm, not first-line). No competing defensible answer; beta-blockade remains standard despite hypotension since instability is driven by the high-output adrenergic tachyarrhythmia. No iodine/Wolff-Chaikoff sequencing trap present. Reference: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/hyperthyroidism/ resolves with substantive on-topic content explicitly covering thyroid storm as a perioperative life-threat and its multimodal treatment (propranolol with peripheral-conversion inhibition, methimazole/PTU, corticosteroids, cooling). Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 58-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis treated with prednisone 30 mg daily for the past 4 months presents for elective total knee arthroplasty under spinal anesthesia. He took his usual morning prednisone. Roughly 90 minutes into the case his blood pressure drops to 72/40 mmHg and remains there despite 1.5 L of balanced crystalloid and escalating phenylephrine and norepinephrine infusions. The block level is stable at T8, there is minimal surgical blood loss, and rhythm, end-tidal CO2, and airway pressures are unremarkable. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Administer a large additional crystalloid bolus and continue increasing the vasopressor infusions
choices.rationale: Refractoriness to adequate fluids and two vasopressors is precisely the clue that a glucocorticoid deficit, not hypovolemia or sympathectomy, is driving the hypotension.

choices.text: Give intravenous calcium chloride for presumed vasoplegia
choices.rationale: Calcium does not address the suspected adrenal cause and would not be expected to reverse hypotension that is refractory to volume and catecholamines.

choices.text: Convert to general anesthesia, assuming an excessively high spinal block is the cause
choices.rationale: The block level is stable at T8 and the respiratory and hemodynamic picture does not fit a high spinal, so converting the anesthetic does not address the underlying problem.

choices.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone for suspected perioperative adrenal insufficiency
choices.rationale: Chronic high-dose steroids suppress the HPA axis, and refractory hypotension despite fluids and vasopressors is the hallmark of an adrenal crisis treated with stress-dose hydrocortisone.

choices.text: Start a vasopressin infusion as the definitive treatment and take no further diagnostic steps
choices.rationale: Vasopressin may transiently raise pressure but does not correct the underlying glucocorticoid deficiency, which must be treated with hydrocortisone.

explanation: A patient on roughly 20 mg/day or more of prednisone equivalent for over three weeks should be assumed to have a suppressed hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and is at risk for an intraoperative adrenal crisis when the surgical stress response cannot be mounted. The classic signal is hypotension that persists despite adequate fluid resuscitation and escalating vasopressors; the indicated treatment is stress-dose hydrocortisone, which provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity. Taking the usual morning dose does not substitute for the higher perioperative requirement during major surgery.
subtopic: Perioperative adrenal insufficiency and corticosteroid stress dosing
reference_title: Adrenal Insufficiency and Perioperative Corticosteroids - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: Keyed answer (IV hydrocortisone) is the single best answer. Patient on prednisone 30 mg/day x4 months has tertiary HPA suppression (well past the >=20 mg, >=3 week threshold); refractory hypotension despite adequate fluids and two catecholamine infusions, with a stable T8 block, minimal blood loss, and normal rhythm/EtCO2/airway pressures excluding competing causes, is the classic hallmark of intraoperative adrenal crisis. All distractors are genuinely inferior: more fluid/pressors already failed; calcium does not address the adrenal cause; high-spinal conversion is excluded by the stable T8 block and normal respiratory picture; vasopressin "as definitive treatment with no further steps" ignores the glucocorticoid deficiency. Stem is unambiguous and internally consistent. One minor explanatory nuance: the claim that hydrocortisone supplies needed mineralocorticoid activity is technically true (cortisol has intrinsic mineralocorticoid effect) but slightly tangential since aldosterone is typically preserved in steroid-induced/tertiary suppression — this does not affect the answer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves (last updated 05/24/2024) and is directly on-topic, covering HPA suppression from exogenous steroids, adrenal crisis presenting with instability refractory to fluids/vasopressors, the >=5 mg/>=20 mg dosing thresholds, and hydrocortisone as first-line stress-dose therapy. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 5-week-old, full-term boy (4.6 kg) is scheduled for a Ramstedt pyloromyotomy after a 2-week history of progressive nonbilious projectile vomiting. He appears lethargic with dry mucous membranes and a sunken anterior fontanelle. Admission labs: Na+ 132 mEq/L, K+ 2.9 mEq/L, Cl- 88 mEq/L, HCO3- 36 mEq/L, pH 7.52. The surgeon asks to bring the infant to the operating room now so the case is not delayed. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Proceed immediately to the operating room and correct electrolytes intraoperatively, since pyloromyotomy is a surgical emergency
choices.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is a medical urgency but never a surgical emergency, and operating before correcting the hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis exposes the infant to dysrhythmias and postoperative apnea.

choices.text: Delay surgery and resuscitate with intravenous isotonic fluid and potassium chloride until the chloride and bicarbonate normalize
choices.rationale: Correct: volume and electrolyte resuscitation (typically until Cl- exceeds ~100 mEq/L, HCO3- falls below ~30 mEq/L, and K+ normalizes) is required before anesthesia because the alkalosis and hypovolemia are the true threats.

choices.text: Administer intravenous acetazolamide to rapidly correct the metabolic alkalosis before induction
choices.rationale: Acetazolamide is not indicated here because the alkalosis is chloride-responsive and resolves with chloride and volume repletion, while acetazolamide would worsen the existing potassium depletion.

choices.text: Begin a hyperventilation strategy after induction to compensate for the metabolic alkalosis
choices.rationale: Hyperventilation would superimpose a respiratory alkalosis and does nothing to correct the underlying volume and electrolyte deficits that endanger the infant.

choices.text: Resuscitate with 0.45% saline boluses to avoid sodium overload in a small infant
choices.rationale: Hypotonic fluid is inappropriate for acute resuscitation because it provides insufficient chloride to reverse the alkalosis and risks hyponatremia; isotonic chloride-containing fluid is required.

explanation: Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis produces a hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis from loss of gastric HCl, compounded by dehydration. Because it is a medical urgency rather than a surgical emergency, anesthesia must be deferred until fluid and electrolytes are corrected with isotonic chloride-containing fluid plus potassium, generally targeting Cl- > 100 mEq/L, HCO3- < 30 mEq/L, and a normal potassium. Operating on an alkalotic, hypovolemic infant invites intraoperative dysrhythmias and an exaggerated risk of postoperative central apnea from a CSF alkalosis that blunts the respiratory drive.
subtopic: Neonatal/infant fluid, electrolyte, and acid-base management (pyloric stenosis)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Classic infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis with textbook hypochloremic (Cl- 88), hypokalemic (K+ 2.9) metabolic alkalosis (HCO3- 36, pH 7.52) plus clinical dehydration. Keyed answer B (delay surgery; resuscitate with isotonic chloride-containing fluid + KCl until electrolytes normalize) is the universally accepted, single best management. Pyloric stenosis is a medical urgency, never a surgical emergency (option A is a classic wrong answer). All distractors are genuinely inferior: acetazolamide is contraindicated in chloride-responsive alkalosis and worsens hypokalemia; hyperventilation only adds respiratory alkalosis without correcting deficits; 0.45% saline provides insufficient chloride and risks hyponatremia. The cited resuscitation targets (Cl- >100, HCO3- <30, normal K+) are standard pediatric anesthesia teaching, and the postoperative central apnea risk from CSF alkalosis blunting respiratory drive is a recognized, accurate teaching point. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK555931/ resolves to 'Pyloric Stenosis - StatPearls,' directly on-topic, confirming the hypochloremic/hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, the need to correct fluids/electrolytes before surgery, and the medical-urgency-not-surgical-emergency framing.

stem: A 3-year-old, 15 kg girl undergoes an inhalational induction with sevoflurane for adenotonsillectomy. As the surgeon places the mouth gag, before any airway device is inserted, the child develops complete airway obstruction with no chest movement, no breath sounds, and absent capnography despite vigorous bag-mask attempts. SpO2 falls from 99% to 88%. There is no intravenous access yet. After delivering 100% oxygen with a tight mask seal, applying jaw thrust, and pressing firmly at the laryngospasm notch, the obstruction persists and SpO2 is now 80% and falling. What is the most appropriate next intervention?

choices.text: Administer intramuscular succinylcholine 4 mg/kg now
choices.rationale: Correct: with no IV access and refractory laryngospasm progressing to hypoxia, intramuscular succinylcholine (about 4 mg/kg) rapidly relaxes the cords and is the definitive rescue when first-line maneuvers fail.

choices.text: Attempt immediate direct laryngoscopy and forceful endotracheal intubation through the closed cords
choices.rationale: Forcing a tube past tightly adducted, spasming cords risks airway trauma and is far less reliable than pharmacologic relaxation in a hypoxemic child.

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia by increasing the sevoflurane concentration and continue bag-mask ventilation
choices.rationale: Deepening the volatile may help early spasm, but with no effective ventilation and SpO2 at 80% the agent cannot be delivered to the lungs, so this wastes critical time.

choices.text: Establish intravenous access first, then give propofol 1 mg/kg to break the spasm
choices.rationale: Stopping to obtain IV access during worsening hypoxia is dangerous, and propofol is less reliable than a muscle relaxant for true complete laryngospasm.

choices.text: Administer intramuscular atropine 0.02 mg/kg as the initial drug to relieve the laryngospasm
choices.rationale: Atropine treats or prevents the bradycardia that may follow hypoxia or succinylcholine but does nothing to relax the laryngeal musculature causing the obstruction.

explanation: Laryngospasm is most common in young children during light planes of anesthesia, particularly with airway stimulation. First-line management is 100% oxygen, a tight mask seal with CPAP, jaw thrust, and firm pressure at the laryngospasm (Larson) notch. When spasm is complete and refractory with falling saturation and no IV in place, intramuscular succinylcholine roughly 4 mg/kg is the definitive rescue; atropine is given alongside or beforehand to blunt the bradycardia that hypoxia and succinylcholine can provoke in small children.
subtopic: Pediatric airway emergencies (laryngospasm)

verdict.reason: Clinical: Keyed answer (IM succinylcholine ~4 mg/kg) is the single best answer for refractory complete laryngospasm with no IV access and progressive hypoxia (SpO2 80% and falling) — the textbook gold-standard rescue, confirmed by OpenAnesthesia (3-4 mg/kg IM) and the broader literature. All distractors are genuinely inferior: forceful intubation through adducted cords risks trauma and is unreliable; deepening sevoflurane is futile when there is no effective ventilation to deliver agent; stopping to obtain IV access wastes time during worsening hypoxia and propofol is less reliable for true complete spasm; IM atropine does not relax laryngeal muscle. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current, including the correct framing of atropine as an adjunct to blunt bradycardia (not the primary rescue) and the standard first-line sequence (100% O2, tight seal/CPAP, jaw thrust, Larson/laryngospasm notch pressure). Stem is unambiguous — it specifies complete obstruction, refractoriness to first-line maneuvers, no IV, and falling saturation, cleanly excluding the deepen-anesthesia and obtain-IV-first options. Reference: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/laryngospasm/ resolves and loads; the page is directly on-topic (titled 'Laryngospasm'), explicitly supports IM succinylcholine 3-4 mg/kg when no IV access, describes the Larson maneuver, and covers pediatric airway risk. Title matches the page. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 3.2 kg ex-premature infant born at 30 weeks gestation, now 42 weeks postmenstrual age, is scheduled for elective unilateral inguinal hernia repair. He has been feeding well and has no apnea on the ward, and a recent hematocrit is 34%. The parents and the ambulatory surgery coordinator ask whether the infant can be discharged home a few hours after an uneventful general anesthetic. Which recommendation is most appropriate?

choices.text: Discharge home the same day as planned, because he is a healthy, thriving, full-feeding infant with a normal hematocrit
choices.rationale: Same-day discharge is unsafe because postoperative apnea can occur hours after an apparently uneventful anesthetic in a former preterm infant below the accepted postmenstrual-age threshold.

choices.text: Admit for at least 12 hours of continuous postoperative apnea monitoring because his postmenstrual age is below the threshold considered safe for outpatient surgery
choices.rationale: Correct: a former preterm infant at 42 weeks postmenstrual age is well under the commonly used ~60-week cutoff, so apnea monitoring (often >= 12 hours apnea-free) is indicated before discharge.

choices.text: Proceed as outpatient surgery but discharge once the infant has been apnea-free for 2 hours in the PACU
choices.rationale: A brief 2-hour observation is inadequate because clinically significant apnea may first appear up to 12 hours postoperatively in this age group.

choices.text: Cancel surgery until the infant reaches 44 weeks postmenstrual age, after which outpatient repair carries no apnea risk
choices.rationale: Apnea risk persists well beyond 44 weeks in former preterm infants, and routinely canceling an indicated incarceration-prone hernia repair is neither necessary nor evidence-based.

choices.text: Perform the repair under spinal anesthesia to eliminate the need for postoperative apnea monitoring
choices.rationale: A spinal technique lowers but does not abolish apnea risk, especially if any sedation is added, so postoperative monitoring is still required in an at-risk infant.

explanation: Former preterm infants are at substantially increased risk of postoperative apnea, bradycardia, and desaturation after general anesthesia, and the risk falls with advancing postmenstrual age. Most institutions admit any former preterm infant below roughly 60 weeks postmenstrual age for at least 12 hours of continuous apnea monitoring, even after an uneventful case and even with regional techniques, because apnea may be delayed. Anemia and ongoing apnea spells further raise risk. A 42-week postmenstrual-age ex-30-weeker therefore is not a candidate for same-day discharge.
subtopic: Prematurity and postoperative apnea / ambulatory disposition
reference_title: Apnea of Prematurity and Postoperative Apnea - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 1 — admit for >=12 hours of postoperative apnea monitoring) is the single best answer and is unambiguous. A former 30-week preterm infant at 42 weeks PMA falls below every published outpatient-discharge threshold (50, 55, or 60 weeks), so same-day discharge (A) is unsafe and 2-hour PACU observation (C) is inadequate because apnea can be delayed up to ~12 hours. Choice D is wrong on two counts (apnea risk persists well beyond 44 weeks; routinely canceling an incarceration-prone hernia repair is not standard) and E is wrong because regional/spinal reduces but does not eliminate apnea risk, especially with any sedation. The stem deliberately gives Hct 34% (above the <30% anemia threshold) and no apnea spells to isolate PMA as the sole driver, keeping the case internally consistent; the explanation's mention of anemia is a general statement, not a claim about this infant, so it is not misleading. Terminology (PMA/PCA) and the 12-hour figure match standard teaching. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/apnea-of-prematurity-and-postoperative-apnea/ resolves successfully and is directly on-topic — it covers postoperative apnea in former preterm infants, the <50/55-60-week PCA thresholds, the >=12-hour monitoring requirement, anemia (Hct <30%) as a risk factor, and that regional anesthesia does not eliminate the need for monitoring. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 67-year-old man is scheduled for an elective right total knee arthroplasty. His history includes insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes mellitus with a hemoglobin A1c of 7.4%, hypertension controlled on lisinopril, and stable angina for which he uses sublingual nitroglycerin roughly once a month with no recent change in pattern. He climbs two flights of stairs and gardens without chest pain or dyspnea. He does not have decompensated heart failure, recent myocardial infarction, or unstable symptoms. Vitals are blood pressure 138/82 mm Hg, heart rate 74/min, SpO2 98% on room air. According to the American Society of Anesthesiologists Physical Status Classification System, which class most accurately describes this patient?

choices.text: ASA I
choices.rationale: ASA I is reserved for a normal healthy patient with no systemic disease, which this man with diabetes, hypertension, and angina clearly is not.

choices.text: ASA II
choices.rationale: ASA II denotes mild systemic disease without substantive functional limitation, but stable angina represents a severe systemic disease that exceeds the 'mild' threshold.

choices.text: ASA III
choices.rationale: ASA III applies to severe systemic disease that is not an immediate threat to life, which fits insulin-dependent diabetes plus stable but symptomatic coronary artery disease with preserved functional capacity.

choices.text: ASA IV
choices.rationale: ASA IV requires severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life, such as recent MI, unstable angina, or decompensated heart failure, none of which this stable patient exhibits.

choices.text: ASA V
choices.rationale: ASA V designates a moribund patient not expected to survive 24 hours without the operation, which is incompatible with an ambulatory patient presenting for elective arthroplasty.

explanation: ASA physical status is assigned from systemic disease burden and its threat to life, independent of the surgical procedure. Insulin-dependent diabetes and symptomatic coronary artery disease (stable angina) are ASA-provided examples of severe systemic disease, placing this patient at ASA III; the disease is severe but not a constant threat to life, which would be required for ASA IV. The classification reflects baseline physiology rather than functional capacity per se, so good exercise tolerance does not downgrade an ASA III patient to II.
subtopic: ASA physical status classification
reference_title: American Society of Anesthesiologists Physical Status Classification System (StatPearls)

verdict.reason: Clinical: Keyed answer ASA III (index 2) is unambiguously correct. The official ASA Physical Status examples (2014, amended 2020) explicitly list "stable angina" and "poorly controlled DM" as ASA III examples; symptomatic coronary artery disease (stable angina) alone places this patient at III regardless of preserved exercise tolerance. ASA II is excluded because stable angina exceeds the "mild systemic disease" threshold (ASA II = well-controlled DM/HTN). ASA IV is correctly excluded because it requires a constant threat to life (recent <3mo MI, unstable angina, decompensated HF, ongoing ischemia), none present. ASA I and V rationales are accurate. All five distractor rationales are correct and the distractors are genuinely inferior. The explanation's claim that good functional capacity does not downgrade an ASA III patient is a defensible simplification consistent with the listed-example approach; minor imprecision but does not affect the key. Reference: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441940/ resolves (HTTP 200) and is the StatPearls "ASA Physical Status Classification System" article — precisely on-topic, current, and an appropriate citation. Both clinical content and reference are sound.

stem: A 52-year-old woman with obstructive sleep apnea and a body mass index of 41 kg/m2 presents for laparoscopic cholecystectomy. During the preoperative airway examination she sits upright, opens her mouth maximally, and protrudes her tongue without phonating. The examiner can see only the soft palate and the base of the uvula. She has a thyromental distance of 5.5 cm, a thick neck, and limited cervical extension. Based on the modified Mallampati assessment performed in this position, which statement most appropriately guides airway planning?

choices.text: The view corresponds to Class I, a reassuring airway that reliably excludes difficult laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Class I (full view of soft palate, uvula, and tonsillar pillars) does not match a view limited to soft palate and uvular base, and no single test reliably excludes a difficult airway.

choices.text: The view corresponds to Class III, but this score alone has low positive predictive value and must be integrated with other predictors such as thyromental distance, neck mobility, and the 3-3-2 rule
choices.rationale: Visibility of only the soft palate and base of the uvula is Class III, yet the Mallampati test has a sensitivity near 0.5 and PPV reported as low as roughly 33%, so it cannot stand alone and must be combined with other airway predictors.

choices.text: The view corresponds to Class IV, which mandates immediate awake fiberoptic intubation regardless of other findings
choices.rationale: Class IV is restricted to visualization of the hard palate only, which does not fit this examination, and management is never dictated by Mallampati class in isolation.

choices.text: The view corresponds to Class II, reassuring enough that no further airway assessment is warranted
choices.rationale: Class II shows the soft palate and uvula with the pillars obscured, a more favorable view than described, and additional predictors should always be integrated.

choices.text: The view corresponds to Class III and, taken alone, confirms that direct laryngoscopy will fail
choices.rationale: Although the view is correctly Class III, the test's low positive predictive value means it cannot by itself confirm that laryngoscopy will fail.

explanation: The Mallampati test must be performed sitting, with the mouth maximally open, tongue protruded, and WITHOUT phonation, because vocalizing falsely improves the apparent class; a view of only the soft palate and base of the uvula is Class III. Even when done correctly, the modified Mallampati has a sensitivity around 0.5 and a positive predictive value reported as low as roughly 33%, so it cannot confirm or exclude a difficult airway on its own.
