Best practice integrates it with thyromental distance, mouth opening, neck extension, and the 3-3-2 rule, especially in a patient with obesity, OSA, a short thyromental distance, and limited cervical extension.
subtopic: Preoperative airway assessment and prediction of difficult intubation

verdict.reason: CLINICAL (sound): The keyed answer (B) is the single best answer. A view of only the soft palate and base of the uvula is correctly Class III, which the StatPearls reference confirms verbatim ("Soft palate and uvula base visible"). The distractors are genuinely inferior: A (Class I = full view incl. tonsillar pillars) and D (Class II = pillars obscured) assign the wrong class and offer false reassurance; C wrongly maps the view to Class IV (which is hard-palate-only per the reference) and adds a false absolute; E correctly identifies Class III but overclaims that it "confirms laryngoscopy will fail," contradicted by the test's low PPV. The cited statistics are accurate and current: sensitivity ~0.5 (reference: Cochrane 0.51) and PPV as low as ~33% (reference: 33.3%; 22% for Class III). The phonation teaching point is correct (reference confirms the test is performed without phonation, and vocalizing falsely improves the apparent class). The 3-3-2 rule and integration with thyromental distance and neck mobility reflect standard current practice; the stem's TMD of 5.5 cm and OSA/obesity/limited extension are internally consistent risk modifiers. REFERENCE (sound): WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK585119/ resolves to the StatPearls "Mallampati Score" article, which is precisely on-topic and corroborates the technique, all four class definitions, and the diagnostic-accuracy figures used. Both checks pass.

stem: A healthy 30-year-old woman is scheduled for an elective outpatient knee arthroscopy under general anesthesia. She has no gastroesophageal reflux, no diabetes, and is on no medications that delay gastric emptying. She tells the preoperative nurse that she had a glass of clear apple juice 3 hours ago, a cup of black coffee 3 hours ago, and her last solid meal (toast) was 7 hours ago. Following standard ASA preoperative fasting recommendations for healthy patients undergoing elective procedures, what is the most appropriate course of action?

choices.text: Cancel the case for the day because any oral intake within 8 hours precludes elective general anesthesia
choices.rationale: An 8-hour fast is required only after a heavy or fatty meal, not after clear liquids or a light solid, so a blanket cancellation is overly conservative.

choices.text: Delay until 6 hours have elapsed from the clear liquids before inducing anesthesia
choices.rationale: Clear liquids require only a 2-hour fast, not 6 hours, so this needlessly delays an appropriately fasted patient.

choices.text: Proceed immediately and additionally give routine prophylactic metoclopramide and an H2 blocker to all such patients
choices.rationale: While the case may proceed, ASA guidance does not endorse routine pharmacologic aspiration prophylaxis in healthy patients without specific risk factors.

choices.text: Delay because black coffee containing no milk is treated as a solid and mandates an 8-hour fast
choices.rationale: Black coffee without milk or solids is classified as a clear liquid with a 2-hour minimum, not a solid, so this rationale is incorrect.

choices.text: Proceed now, because clear liquids require only a 2-hour fast while a light solid meal requires 6 hours, and both intervals have been satisfied
choices.rationale: Clear liquids (apple juice, black coffee) have a 2-hour minimum and toast as a light meal has a 6-hour minimum, both exceeded at 3 and 7 hours respectively, so the case may proceed.

explanation: ASA fasting guidelines for healthy patients undergoing elective surgery specify a minimum of 2 hours after clear liquids, 4 hours after breast milk, 6 hours after a light meal or non-human milk/formula, and 8 hours after a fried, fatty, or heavy meal. Black coffee and clear juice without pulp are clear liquids, while toast is a light solid meal. With 3 hours since clear liquids and 7 hours since a light meal, this patient satisfies both thresholds and may proceed. Routine pharmacologic aspiration prophylaxis is not recommended in healthy patients without specific risk factors.
subtopic: Aspiration risk and preoperative fasting guidelines

verdict.reason: Clinical content is correct and unambiguous. Keyed answer (index 4) matches standard ASA preoperative fasting guidelines: clear liquids 2h (apple juice without pulp and black coffee without milk are both clear liquids; satisfied at 3h), light meal/non-human milk 6h (toast is the canonical light meal; satisfied at 7h), with 4h for breast milk and 8h for fried/fatty/heavy meals. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: 8h blanket requirement (only for heavy meals), 6h for clear liquids (should be 2h), routine pharmacologic aspiration prophylaxis not endorsed in healthy low-risk patients, and the false premise that black coffee is a solid. No second defensible answer. Reference: WebFetch resolved successfully to the genuine StatPearls 'Aspiration Risk' chapter (NBK470169), matching reference_title; it is on-topic for aspiration risk/NPO and discusses keeping patients NPO, though it does not enumerate the exact ASA interval table. It is a real, relevant, non-fabricated citation, so it does not constitute a reference problem.

stem: A 34-year-old man with a complete spinal cord injury at T4 sustained 2 years ago presents for cystoscopy under monitored anesthesia care with light sedation. Twenty minutes into bladder irrigation, his blood pressure abruptly rises from 118/74 to 212/118 mmHg and his heart rate falls from 78 to 46 beats/min. He reports a pounding headache, and the skin above his clavicles appears flushed and diaphoretic while his lower extremities are cool and pale. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate next step?

choices.text: Stop the bladder irrigation and drain the bladder while raising the head of the bed
choices.rationale: Removing the inciting noxious stimulus below the injury level and using upright positioning to promote orthostatic blood pressure reduction is the first-line maneuver in autonomic dysreflexia and frequently aborts the crisis.

choices.text: Administer intravenous atropine to treat the bradycardia
choices.rationale: The bradycardia is a baroreceptor-mediated reflex response to severe hypertension, not the primary problem, so treating heart rate without addressing the blood pressure and trigger is misguided.

choices.text: Give a phenylephrine bolus to support perfusion pressure
choices.rationale: Phenylephrine is a vasopressor that would dangerously worsen the already severe hypertension of autonomic dysreflexia and is contraindicated here.

choices.text: Place the patient in steep Trendelenburg position
choices.rationale: Trendelenburg raises central and cerebral pressures and would aggravate the hypertensive emergency; the head should be elevated, not lowered.

choices.text: Deepen sedation with a propofol infusion and continue the procedure
choices.rationale: Although deepening anesthesia can blunt the response, sedation alone without removing the trigger fails to address the cause and risks ongoing life-threatening hypertension and cerebral hemorrhage.

explanation: Autonomic dysreflexia is a syndrome of unchecked sympathetic discharge below the level of a spinal cord injury at or above T6, classically triggered by bladder or bowel distension, producing severe hypertension with reflex bradycardia, headache, and flushing above the lesion. The cornerstone of acute management is immediate removal of the inciting stimulus (drain the bladder, stop irrigation) and sitting the patient upright to lower blood pressure; pharmacologic agents such as nitroglycerin or nifedipine are added only if hypertension persists. Vasopressors and Trendelenburg are contraindicated because they worsen the hypertensive crisis.
subtopic: Autonomic dysreflexia in chronic spinal cord injury

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Stem is a textbook autonomic dysreflexia presentation — complete SCI at T4 (above the T6 threshold), bladder distension from cystoscopy/irrigation as trigger, severe hypertension (212/118), reflex bradycardia (46), pounding headache, and flushing/diaphoresis above the lesion with cool/pale lower extremities. Keyed answer (index 0: stop irrigation, drain bladder, raise head of bed) is the single best immediate step and matches all standard guidance — removing the noxious stimulus below the lesion is the most important first action, and head elevation lowers BP via gravitational venous pooling. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: atropine treats a reflex bradycardia that is not the primary problem; phenylephrine is a vasopressor contraindicated in a hypertensive crisis; steep Trendelenburg worsens cerebral pressures (opposite of indicated); deepening sedation while continuing the procedure leaves the trigger in place. The explanation correctly states the T6 threshold, pathophysiology, and the management hierarchy (remove stimulus + sit up first, then nitroglycerin/nifedipine if persistent). Minor nuance: the keyed rationale's phrase 'orthostatic blood pressure reduction' is slightly loose physiology in a patient with impaired orthostatic reflexes, but the maneuver (head up) and its BP-lowering effect are correct and standard — not a clinical error. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/autonomic-dysreflexia/ resolves successfully and is directly on-topic — it covers AD triggers (bladder distension), the hypertension-with-reflex-bradycardia picture, and acute management including removing the inciting stimulus. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 58-year-old woman is undergoing a posterior fossa craniotomy in the sitting position for resection of a vestibular schwannoma. She is monitored with a precordial Doppler, end-tidal CO2, and a right atrial catheter. During tumor dissection near a venous sinus, the anesthetist hears a sudden mill-wheel/roaring change in the Doppler tone, the end-tidal CO2 drops from 34 to 19 mmHg over 30 seconds, and the blood pressure begins to fall. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.text: Increase the inspired oxygen concentration to 100% and reduce minute ventilation
choices.rationale: Although raising FiO2 to 100% is appropriate, reducing minute ventilation is not a priority maneuver and does not address ongoing air entrainment, so this is not the best immediate action.

choices.text: Notify the surgeon to flood the field with saline and apply jugular venous compression
choices.rationale: Alerting the surgeon to occlude the open vein (irrigating the field and compressing the jugular veins to raise venous pressure and identify the entry site) directly stops further air entrainment, the essential first step in venous air embolism.

choices.text: Immediately discontinue nitrous oxide and start a norepinephrine infusion
choices.rationale: Stopping nitrous oxide is correct and a vasopressor may be needed, but neither halts continued air entry, which must be addressed first by closing the source.

choices.text: Aspirate from the right atrial catheter and then reposition the patient supine
choices.rationale: Right atrial aspiration is a valid adjunct, but full supine repositioning is impractical during active craniotomy and delays the critical action of stopping entrainment at the surgical site.

choices.text: Begin rapid crystalloid bolus and place the patient in steep reverse Trendelenburg
choices.rationale: Reverse Trendelenburg lowers venous pressure at the head and would promote further air entrainment, making it harmful in this setting.

explanation: Venous air embolism is a feared complication of sitting craniotomy, with reported incidence as high as 76%, because the surgical site sits above the heart and non-collapsible venous sinuses can entrain air. A sudden change in precordial Doppler tone with an abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2 is the classic early signature. The first priority is to stop further air entry: alert the surgeon to flood the field and occlude the open vessel, apply bilateral jugular compression to raise cerebral venous pressure and expose the source, discontinue nitrous oxide, give 100% oxygen, and aspirate air from a right atrial catheter while supporting hemodynamics. Lowering the head relative to the heart and reversing the pressure gradient is also helpful, whereas reverse Trendelenburg worsens entrainment.
subtopic: Venous air embolism during sitting-position neurosurgery

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Stem is a textbook venous air embolism (VAE) presentation during sitting-position posterior fossa craniotomy — mill-wheel Doppler change, abrupt EtCO2 drop (34→19), falling BP during dissection near a venous sinus. Keyed answer B (notify surgeon to flood field + apply jugular compression) is the single best immediate action: the cardinal first priority in VAE is to STOP further air entrainment at the source. Flooding the field occludes/identifies the open vessel and bilateral jugular compression raises cerebral venous pressure to both reduce entrainment and expose the bleeding site. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A (100% O2 correct but reducing minute ventilation is not a priority and ignores ongoing entry); C (stopping N2O + norepinephrine are adjuncts that don't halt air entry); D (right atrial aspiration is a valid concurrent adjunct but only removes air already entrained, and full supine repositioning mid-craniotomy is impractical/delaying); E (reverse Trendelenburg lowers head venous pressure and worsens entrainment — actively harmful). The board convention of prioritizing source control over RA aspiration makes B unambiguously best over D. Explanation is accurate and current, including the 15–76% incidence range. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/venous-air-embolism/ resolves and is directly on-topic — it covers sitting craniotomy incidence (15–76%, matching the stated 76%), precordial Doppler and falling EtCO2 detection, and the management hierarchy of preventing additional air entry first (notify surgeon to flood field, discontinue N2O, 100% O2, RA aspiration, vasopressor support). Citation is accurate and supports the keyed answer. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 27-year-old man is brought to the operating room for evacuation of an acute subdural hematoma after a motor vehicle collision. His Glasgow Coma Scale is 6, he is intubated, and an intracranial pressure monitor reads 24 mmHg. His mean arterial pressure is 82 mmHg. The neurosurgeon asks the anesthetist to optimize ventilation. Arterial blood gas shows a PaCO2 of 38 mmHg. Which of the following ventilation strategies is most appropriate at this time?

choices.text: Maintain PaCO2 in the normocapnic range of approximately 35-40 mmHg
choices.rationale: Targeting normocapnia preserves adequate cerebral blood flow while avoiding the ischemic risk of aggressive hyperventilation, and is the recommended baseline ventilation goal in traumatic brain injury.

choices.text: Institute sustained hyperventilation to a PaCO2 of 25 mmHg for the duration of the case
choices.rationale: Prolonged aggressive hyperventilation to a PaCO2 of 25 mmHg causes cerebral vasoconstriction severe enough to risk ischemia and is specifically discouraged as a routine strategy.

choices.text: Allow permissive hypercapnia with a PaCO2 of 50-55 mmHg to limit barotrauma
choices.rationale: Hypercapnia causes cerebral vasodilation that raises cerebral blood volume and intracranial pressure, which is dangerous in a patient with an already elevated ICP.

choices.text: Target a PaCO2 of 45 mmHg to maximize cerebral oxygen delivery
choices.rationale: Mild hypercapnia increases cerebral blood volume and ICP without a meaningful benefit and is inappropriate in the setting of intracranial hypertension.

choices.text: Briefly hyperventilate to a PaCO2 below 20 mmHg as a standing intraoperative goal
choices.rationale: Driving PaCO2 below 20 mmHg produces extreme vasoconstriction and ischemia and is never an appropriate sustained target, even acutely.

explanation: In traumatic brain injury, cerebral blood flow is tightly coupled to PaCO2, with cerebral blood flow changing roughly 3-4% for every 1 mmHg change in PaCO2. The default ventilation goal is normocapnia (about 35-40 mmHg), which maintains cerebral perfusion while avoiding the rise in cerebral blood volume and ICP that accompanies hypercapnia. Brief, moderate hyperventilation can be used as a temporizing measure for acute herniation, but prolonged or aggressive hyperventilation (PaCO2 below 25-30 mmHg) is avoided because the resulting vasoconstriction can cause cerebral ischemia.
subtopic: CO2 reactivity and ventilation targets in traumatic brain injury

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (maintain normocapnia 35-40 mmHg) is the single best answer and is current per Brain Trauma Foundation guidance. The patient has mildly elevated ICP (24) with adequate CPP (~58) and NO herniation signs, and is already normocapnic at PaCO2 38 — so maintaining normocapnia is unambiguously correct; prophylactic/sustained hyperventilation is avoided. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: sustained hyperventilation to 25 (ischemia risk, explicitly discouraged), permissive hypercapnia 50-55 (raises CBV/ICP), mild hypercapnia 45 (raises ICP, no benefit), and PaCO2 <20 as a standing goal (extreme vasoconstriction). Each rationale is accurate. The explanation correctly cites CBF changing 3-4%/mmHg PaCO2 and correctly frames brief hyperventilation as a temporizing measure for herniation only. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/traumatic-brain-injury/ resolves (not a 404) and is directly on-topic — it explicitly states '3-4% CBF change per 1 mmHg PaCO2' and 'prolonged hyperventilation (PaCO2 <25 mmHg) is not recommended,' matching the question's teaching points. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 32-year-old woman at 37 weeks' gestation (G2P1, BMI 34) is brought to the operating room for a non-urgent repeat cesarean delivery. After an uneventful spinal anesthetic with hyperbaric bupivacaine, fentanyl, and morphine, she is positioned flat and supine while the team preps and drapes. Over the next several minutes she becomes nauseated, pale, and lightheaded; her blood pressure falls from 124/76 to 78/40 mmHg and her heart rate rises to 118/min. The fetal heart-rate tracing shows new prolonged decelerations. Which intervention should be performed FIRST?

choices.text: Administer a 1 g intravenous bolus of calcium gluconate
choices.rationale: Calcium is used for magnesium toxicity or hyperkalemia, not for positional maternal hypotension, and addresses none of this patient's physiology.

choices.text: Give 4 mg of ondansetron to treat the nausea
choices.rationale: Treating the nausea symptomatically ignores the underlying hemodynamic cause and would delay restoring uteroplacental perfusion.

choices.text: Place the patient in steep reverse Trendelenburg
choices.rationale: Reverse Trendelenburg lowers venous return further and can worsen hypotension after a sympathectomy, aggravating the problem.

choices.text: Intubate the trachea and convert to general anesthesia
choices.rationale: This is an unnecessary escalation for reversible positional hypotension and does not address aortocaval compression.

choices.text: Apply left uterine displacement (manually or with a 15-degree leftward table tilt)
choices.rationale: Left uterine displacement relieves compression of the inferior vena cava and aorta by the gravid uterus, restoring venous return, maternal blood pressure, and uteroplacental flow.

explanation: After 20 weeks' gestation the supine position allows the gravid uterus to compress the inferior vena cava and aorta (aortocaval compression / supine hypotensive syndrome), reducing venous return and cardiac output; a fresh spinal sympathectomy magnifies the effect, producing maternal hypotension and fetal compromise. The immediate, definitive maneuver is left uterine displacement by manual displacement or a 15-degree left table tilt, which mechanically relieves the obstruction. Vasopressors and fluids are useful adjuncts, but positioning is the first and most specific step.
subtopic: Maternal physiology — aortocaval compression and left uterine displacement

verdict.reason: Clinical: Stem is a classic aortocaval compression/supine hypotensive syndrome in a 37-week parturient, magnified by a fresh spinal sympathectomy (supine-flat positioning, hypotension 124/76->78/40, reflex tachycardia 118, fetal decelerations). Keyed answer (left uterine displacement, index 4) is the single best and most specific first maneuver, mechanically relieving IVC/aortic compression to restore venous return, maternal BP, and uteroplacental flow. All distractors are genuinely inferior: calcium gluconate (irrelevant; for Mg toxicity/hyperkalemia), ondansetron (treats symptom not cause), reverse Trendelenburg (further reduces venous return after sympathectomy, harmful), and intubation/GA conversion (gross over-escalation). No competing defensible answer is offered (phenylephrine, the other near-simultaneous real-world step, is not a choice), and the explanation correctly frames vasopressors/fluids as adjuncts. Reference: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430759/ resolves to 'Aortocaval Compression Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf,' precisely on-topic, covering >20 weeks gestation, IVC/aortic compression, supine hypotensive syndrome, and 15-degree left tilt / manual leftward uterine displacement as management. Both dimensions sound.

stem: A 29-year-old laboring parturient at term has had a well-functioning lumbar epidural providing a bilateral T10 sensory level for the past 4 hours. The fetal heart tracing abruptly develops a prolonged bradycardia to 70/min that does not recover, and the obstetrician calls for an emergent (category 1) cesarean delivery, aiming for delivery within minutes. The epidural catheter is confirmed to be in good position and the block is symmetric. Maternal vital signs are stable. Which local anesthetic is the BEST choice to rapidly extend the existing epidural to a surgical block?

choices.text: 3% 2-chloroprocaine
choices.rationale: 3% 2-chloroprocaine produces the most rapid surgical-density epidural block (onset ~3-5 minutes), is metabolized almost instantly by plasma ester hydrolysis with minimal placental transfer, making it ideal for category 1 cesarean delivery through an in-situ catheter.

choices.text: 0.5% plain bupivacaine
choices.rationale: Bupivacaine has a slow onset of dense surgical block (often 15-20 minutes) and greater cardiotoxic potential, making it unsuitable when delivery is needed within minutes.

choices.text: 0.125% bupivacaine with fentanyl
choices.rationale: This is a dilute analgesic labor mixture that cannot produce surgical anesthesia at any speed.

choices.text: 0.75% ropivacaine
choices.rationale: Although denser than labor concentrations, ropivacaine still has a substantially slower onset than chloroprocaine and is a poor fit for a delivery measured in minutes.

choices.text: 2% lidocaine without additives
choices.rationale: Plain lidocaine works but has a slower onset (~10-12 minutes) than chloroprocaine, and without bicarbonate/epinephrine it is slower still, making it second-best for a true crash extension.

explanation: When an emergent cesarean is needed and a functioning labor epidural is already in place, extending the existing block is usually faster and safer than performing a new spinal or inducing general anesthesia. 3% 2-chloroprocaine gives the fastest dense surgical block (about 3-5 minutes versus 10-12 minutes for lidocaine), and its rapid ester hydrolysis means minimal fetal exposure and low systemic toxicity. Its short duration and potential interference with epidural opioids are accepted trade-offs in this time-critical setting.
subtopic: Regional technique — local anesthetic selection for emergent epidural extension

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (3% 2-chloroprocaine) is the single best choice and reflects standard OB anesthesia teaching — for a category 1 cesarean with a well-functioning in-situ labor epidural, extending the existing block is preferred, and 3% 2-chloroprocaine gives the fastest dense surgical block via an epidural catheter, with rapid plasma ester hydrolysis minimizing fetal/systemic exposure. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: 0.5% plain bupivacaine (slow, cardiotoxic), 0.125% bupivacaine+fentanyl (analgesic dilution incapable of surgical anesthesia), 0.75% ropivacaine (slower than chloroprocaine), and 2% lidocaine without additives (works but slower). The stem's specification of lidocaine 'without additives' removes the only realistic competitor, so the answer is unambiguous. Minor imprecision: the keyed rationale cites ~3-5 min onset for plain 3% 2-chloroprocaine, whereas the cited source gives ~8 min plain (~3 min with bicarbonate); this does not alter the correct ranking or the best answer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/2-chloroprocaine_onset/ resolves to a genuine OpenAnesthesia article 'Anesthetic Management of Cesarean Delivery' (updated 09/27/2023) that explicitly discusses 2-chloroprocaine onset, epidural extension for cesarean, and local anesthetic onset times — directly on-topic. No reference problem.

stem: A healthy 26-year-old nullipara undergoes elective cesarean delivery under spinal anesthesia with hyperbaric bupivacaine and fentanyl. Shortly after the block sets up, her blood pressure falls from 118/72 to 84/48 mmHg with a heart rate of 92/min; she is normovolemic and left uterine displacement is in place. You plan to support her blood pressure to maintain uteroplacental perfusion and optimize neonatal outcome. Compared with ephedrine, which statement about using a phenylephrine infusion in this setting is correct?

choices.text: Phenylephrine crosses the placenta more readily than ephedrine and directly stimulates fetal beta-receptors
choices.rationale: It is ephedrine, not phenylephrine, that crosses the placenta more and stimulates fetal beta-adrenergic receptors, increasing fetal metabolic rate.

choices.text: Phenylephrine reliably increases maternal heart rate and cardiac output more than ephedrine
choices.rationale: Phenylephrine, a pure alpha-1 agonist, typically causes reflex bradycardia and can modestly lower cardiac output, whereas ephedrine tends to raise heart rate.

choices.text: Phenylephrine is associated with higher umbilical artery pH (less fetal acidosis) than ephedrine when maternal blood pressure is maintained
choices.rationale: Because phenylephrine restores maternal pressure without crossing the placenta to drive fetal beta-stimulation and metabolic acidosis, it preserves fetal acid-base status better than ephedrine.

choices.text: Phenylephrine is contraindicated for spinal hypotension during cesarean delivery and ephedrine is the recommended first-line agent
choices.rationale: This reverses current practice; a phenylephrine infusion is the preferred first-line vasopressor for spinal-induced hypotension in obstetrics.

choices.text: Phenylephrine has no effect on uteroplacental perfusion because uterine vessels are maximally dilated
choices.rationale: Uteroplacental perfusion depends heavily on maintaining maternal mean arterial pressure, so a vasopressor that restores maternal pressure does support uterine blood flow.

explanation: Uteroplacental perfusion is pressure-dependent, so spinal-induced hypotension must be corrected to protect the fetus. Phenylephrine, a pure alpha-1 agonist, restores maternal mean arterial pressure with little placental transfer; ephedrine crosses the placenta and stimulates fetal beta-receptors, raising fetal metabolic rate and lowering umbilical artery pH. Provided maternal cardiac output is maintained, phenylephrine is associated with higher umbilical artery pH (less fetal acidosis) and is the contemporary first-line vasopressor, accepting that its alpha-1 action may produce reflex bradycardia.
subtopic: Uteroplacental pharmacology — vasopressor choice and fetal acid-base status

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 2 — phenylephrine is associated with higher umbilical artery pH / less fetal acidosis than ephedrine when maternal BP is maintained) is the single best answer and is well-supported (Cooper 2002 RCT; Veeser 2012 and Heesen 2014 meta-analyses). All four distractors are unambiguously false: (0) reverses placental transfer/fetal beta-stimulation, which is ephedrine's property; (1) phenylephrine causes reflex bradycardia, not increased HR/CO; (3) reverses current practice — phenylephrine is the 2018 consensus (Kinsella et al.) first-line agent; (4) contradicts the pressure-dependent, non-autoregulated uteroplacental circulation. The stem is unambiguous (healthy, normovolemic, modest hypotension, HR 92 so not bradycardic, LUD in place) and the explanation/rationales are accurate and current; the hedge 'provided maternal cardiac output is maintained' is an appropriate contemporary refinement, not an error. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/uteroplacental-circulation/ resolves successfully and is directly on-topic — it covers pressure-dependence/lack of autoregulation, the perfusion-pressure equation, and explicitly states phenylephrine preserves fetal pH better than ephedrine when cardiac output is maintained. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 34-year-old woman (ASA I) undergoes an uncomplicated outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia at a freestanding ambulatory surgery center. Two hours after arriving in the phase II recovery area she is fully awake and oriented, ambulates to the bathroom with steady gait, and has stable vital signs (BP 122/74, HR 78, RR 14, SpO2 98% on room air). She reports incisional pain of 2/10 controlled with oral acetaminophen-hydrocodone, has had no nausea or vomiting, and her dressings are dry with no bleeding. She has not yet urinated and asks to go home. Her ride is present. Using the Modified Post-Anesthesia Discharge Scoring System (PADSS), what is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Discharge her home now with standard instructions, as voiding is not a mandatory criterion for a low-risk patient
choices.rationale: Correct: in the modified PADSS, mandatory voiding before discharge has been eliminated for low-risk patients without risk factors for urinary retention, so she meets discharge criteria and may go home with appropriate instructions.

choices.text: Mandate spontaneous voiding before discharge and place a straight catheter if she cannot void within 1 hour
choices.rationale: Incorrect: routine mandatory voiding was removed from the modified PADSS for low-risk patients, and reflexive catheterization introduces unnecessary infection and trauma risk.

choices.text: Keep her until she tolerates a full solid meal to confirm she is home-ready
choices.rationale: Incorrect: tolerating oral solids is not a PADSS discharge requirement, and mandatory eating/drinking can delay discharge without improving safety.

choices.text: Administer a 1-liter intravenous fluid bolus to force diuresis and then reassess for voiding
choices.rationale: Incorrect: forcing diuresis can paradoxically overdistend the bladder and provoke retention, and it is not indicated in a hemodynamically stable, euvolemic, low-risk patient.

choices.text: Convert her to a 23-hour observation admission solely because she has not voided
choices.rationale: Incorrect: admission solely for absence of voiding in a low-risk patient is unwarranted and conflicts with modern discharge criteria.

explanation: The modified PADSS scores vital signs, ambulation, nausea/vomiting, pain, and surgical bleeding; a score of 9 or greater (with no single zero) indicates home-readiness. Mandatory documented voiding and mandatory oral intake have been removed for low-risk patients because they delay discharge without improving safety. Voiding should still be confirmed before discharge only in patients with risk factors for retention (e.g., neuraxial anesthesia, anorectal or urologic/hernia surgery, history of retention). This patient meets all PADSS criteria and may be discharged with a responsible adult and clear return precautions.
subtopic: Discharge readiness and PACU discharge criteria (Modified PADSS)

stem: A 58-year-old man (88 kg) undergoes excision of a large dorsal forearm lipoma in an office-based surgical suite under local infiltration. The surgeon injects a total of 45 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine over several minutes. About 8 minutes after the final injection the patient becomes agitated, complains of perioral numbness and ringing in his ears, and then has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure followed by a wide-complex bradycardia with hypotension. Airway and ventilation are being supported with 100% oxygen and a benzodiazepine has been given. In addition to standard resuscitation, which intervention is most specific and appropriate for this condition?

choices.text: Administer a 20% lipid emulsion bolus of approximately 1.5 mL/kg over 1 minute, followed by an infusion
choices.rationale: Correct: this presentation is local anesthetic systemic toxicity, and intravenous lipid emulsion (1.5 mL/kg 20% bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion) is the specific therapy that should be started at the first sign of serious toxicity.

choices.text: Give a standard 1 mg epinephrine bolus as the primary drug, repeated every 3-5 minutes
choices.rationale: Incorrect: in LAST, high-dose epinephrine can impair resuscitation and lipid efficacy, so if epinephrine is used at all it should be in reduced doses (≤1 mcg/kg), not the standard 1 mg ACLS bolus.

choices.text: Administer intravenous lidocaine to suppress the ventricular arrhythmia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: lidocaine is itself a local anesthetic and would add to the sodium-channel blockade driving the toxicity, so it is contraindicated in LAST.

choices.text: Give a calcium channel blocker or beta-blocker to control the rhythm
choices.rationale: Incorrect: calcium channel blockers and beta-blockers are specifically avoided in LAST because they worsen cardiovascular depression and conduction block.

choices.text: Administer flumazenil to reverse the benzodiazepine and treat the seizure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: flumazenil would remove benzodiazepine seizure protection and is irrelevant to the local-anesthetic mechanism, potentially worsening seizures.

explanation: The prodrome of perioral numbness, tinnitus, and agitation progressing to seizure and cardiovascular collapse is classic local anesthetic systemic toxicity, and bupivacaine is the most cardiotoxic of the commonly used agents. Per ASRA guidance, management priorities are airway/oxygenation, seizure suppression with benzodiazepines, and prompt intravenous 20% lipid emulsion (about 1.5 mL/kg bolus, then 0.25 mL/kg/min). Standard ACLS is modified in LAST: epinephrine doses are reduced (≤1 mcg/kg), and vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and additional local anesthetics such as lidocaine are avoided. The 45 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine (225 mg) approached or exceeded the maximum recommended dose, underscoring the importance of dose limits in office-based local infiltration.
subtopic: Office-based anesthesia complication: local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The prodrome (perioral numbness, tinnitus, agitation) progressing to seizure then wide-complex bradycardia/hypotension is textbook LAST, and bupivacaine is the most cardiotoxic common agent. 45 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine = 225 mg, which exceeds the ~175 mg (2 mg/kg) recommended max for plain bupivacaine in an 88 kg patient — the stem's "approached or exceeded" framing is accurate. The keyed answer (20% lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg bolus, then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion) is the single LAST-specific therapy and matches ASRA dosing. All distractors are genuinely inferior/contraindicated per ASRA: standard 1 mg epinephrine bolus (use reduced ≤1 mcg/kg doses), lidocaine (additional Na-channel blocker), CCB/beta-blockers (worsen CV depression/conduction), and flumazenil (removes seizure protection, mechanistically irrelevant). The only nit is the bolus given "over 1 minute" vs the 2020 ASRA checklist's "2-3 minutes," but "~1 minute" is a widely cited, defensible bolus rate and does not affect correctness. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia LAST page resolves, loads, and is precisely on-topic — it covers presentation, lipid emulsion, reduced epinephrine dosing (≤1 mcg/kg), and avoidance of lidocaine/CCBs, corroborating the explanation. Both clinical and reference are sound; keep.

stem: A 78-kg, 24-year-old woman is undergoing an ultrasound-guided axillary brachial plexus block for hand surgery. Approximately 90 seconds after injection of 30 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine, she reports perioral numbness and tinnitus, then has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure and becomes unresponsive. Her rhythm degenerates into a wide-complex tachycardia with a blood pressure of 60/30 mmHg. The seizure is terminated with midazolam and her airway is secured with 100% oxygen. In addition to standard ACLS modifications, which intervention is most specific to the underlying cause and should be initiated immediately?

choices.text: Administer a 1 mg/kg bolus of intravenous lidocaine to stabilize the myocardium
choices.rationale: Lidocaine is itself a sodium-channel-blocking local anesthetic and would worsen, not treat, local anesthetic systemic toxicity.

choices.text: Give a 1.5 mL/kg bolus of 20% lipid emulsion followed by an infusion
choices.rationale: Intravenous lipid emulsion is the targeted antidote for LAST, creating a 'lipid sink' that sequesters bupivacaine and improves cardiac bioenergetics.

choices.text: Administer high-dose epinephrine boluses of 1 mg every 3 minutes per standard ACLS
choices.rationale: Standard high-dose epinephrine is specifically discouraged in LAST because it can impair lipid resuscitation and provoke arrhythmias; small doses (<1 mcg/kg) are preferred.

choices.text: Give intravenous calcium chloride 1 g to counteract the channel blockade
choices.rationale: Calcium does not reverse local-anesthetic sodium-channel blockade and is not part of LAST management.

choices.text: Initiate an amiodarone infusion as the first-line antiarrhythmic
choices.rationale: Amiodarone is not the specific treatment for LAST-induced arrhythmia and does not address the underlying drug toxicity; lipid emulsion is the priority.

explanation: This is classic local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) from bupivacaine, which has high cardiac toxicity due to avid, slowly-dissociating sodium channel blockade. The ASRA practice advisory directs immediate 20% lipid emulsion (1.5 mL/kg lean body weight bolus, then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion), airway/oxygenation, seizure control with benzodiazepines, reduced epinephrine dosing (<1 mcg/kg), and avoidance of lidocaine, vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, and beta-blockers. Bupivacaine arrest can be prolonged, so resuscitation and consideration of cardiopulmonary bypass should be sustained.
subtopic: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) management

All distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: lidocaine is itself a Na-channel blocker that worsens LAST; high-dose epinephrine is discouraged in favor of reduced dosing; calcium does not reverse Na-channel blockade; and amiodarone treats the arrhythmia symptomatically, not the underlying drug toxicity.
